text: The surgeon places four additional 5mm ports in the left upper, left lower, right upper, and right lower quadrants under direct vision. The abdomen is inspected, and the small bowel is swept out of the pelvis.

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon cephalad and laterally to expose the sacral promontory. The peritoneum over the sacral promontory is scored using electrocautery or an advanced energy device, with careful avoidance of large sacral veins and low crossing iliac vessels.

The surgeon continues dissection down the right side of the rectum towards the pelvic floor, carefully avoiding excessive lateral or posterior dissection to preserve the lateral ligaments and hypogastric plexus.

The surgeon carries the dissection anterior onto the rectum a few centimeters above the rectovaginal fold. A piece of biologic mesh, pre-cut to four by twelve centimeters, is introduced through the ten millimeter port. The mesh is secured to the anterior wall of the distal rectum using four to six interrupted polypropylene sutures, taking only seromuscular bites. A digital rectal exam is performed to confirm the sutures are not full thickness and are positioned above the sphincter complex.

The surgeon gently pulls the mesh cephalad and identifies an area on the sacral promontory suitable for reducing the rectum without excess tension. Two 2-0 braided polyester sutures are placed from the presacral fascia to the proximal end of the mesh, with a sliding knot tied extracorporeally to secure the mesh to the sacral promontory.
The surgeon begins at the sacral promontory and closes the peritoneum over the entire mesh using a 2-0 barbed suture, ensuring the mesh is securely covered.
The surgeon closes the fascia of the ten millimeter port under direct vision, ensuring proper closure before removing the remaining surgical ports.
